segment.segmentSummary: In this segment, we introduced the concept of database schema changes, comparing them to house renovations. We focused on non-destructive changes, examining two safe examples: adding a new nullable column and creating an entirely new table. We learned that these changes are safe because they don't break existing application code or invalidate existing data.

screens.title: Two Types of Database Changes
screens.subtitle: Some changes are easy, some are risky.

visualElements.content: The main title and introductory paragraph explaining the two categories of schema changes.
House Renovation Analogy
Non-Destructive = Adding a new shed. The main house is unaffected.
Destructive = Removing a load-bearing wall. Risky! Requires careful planning.
Teacher Note: The analogy helps students build an intuition for the core difference in risk before we introduce the technical details.

blocks.content: Databases Evolve
Just like a house, a database schema isn't static; it needs to change as application requirements evolve. The source text introduces two main categories for these changes.
Every schema change falls into one of two categories: Non-destructive and Destructive.

Analogy: Think of your database as a house. A non-destructive change is like adding a new, separate shed in the backyard. It doesn't disrupt daily life inside the house. A destructive change is like removing a major wall—it's disruptive and requires everyone to coordinate.
In this segment, we'll focus on the safe, non-destructive changes.

boardActions.narrationCue: Let's start by looking at the source text.
It tells us that every change is either non-destructive or destructive.
To understand this, let's use an analogy.
A non-destructive change is like adding a new shed. It adds functionality without disturbing what's already there.
A destructive change, however, is like removing a structural wall. It's much riskier and can cause big problems if not done carefully.

screens.voiceover: Welcome! As applications grow, their databases need to change. Let's start by looking at the source text. It tells us that every schema change falls into one of two categories: non-destructive or destructive. To build an intuition for this, let's use an analogy of renovating a house. A non-destructive change is like adding a new shed in the backyard; it's a new addition that doesn't disrupt the existing house at all. A destructive change, on the other hand, is like removing a load-bearing wall. It's risky, affects the whole structure, and requires careful planning. We're going to start with the safe kind: non-destructive changes.
screens.teacherNote: This screen establishes the core analogy (house renovation) and introduces the key vocabulary (non-destructive vs. destructive) directly from the source text. This provides a mental model for the student to hang the upcoming examples on, following the 'intuition before formalism' principle.
keyPoints: Database schemas must change over time.
Changes are categorized as either non-destructive or destructive.
Non-destructive changes are safe and don't break existing functionality.
Destructive changes are risky and require a careful, multi-phase approach.

sourceRef.quote: Every schema change falls into one of two categories: Non-destructive and Destructive. Destructive changes must be handled with a 3-phase approach.

screens.checkQuestion: Based on the house analogy, why might 'repainting a room' be considered a non-destructive change?

screens.title: Example A: Adding a Nullable Column
screens.subtitle: Our first non-destructive change.

visualElements.content: The user story and SQL code for adding a nullable column.

users (Before)
id | name | email
-- | -- | --
1 | Alice | a@b.com
2 | Bob | c@d.com

users (After)
id | name | email | preferred_language
-- | -- | -- | --
1 | Alice | a@b.com | NULL
2 | Bob | c@d.com | NULL
ALTER TABLE runs
Existing rows get NULL

blocks.content: Let's see a non-destructive change in action.
Example A: Adding a column (Non-destructive)
The user story is to allow users to specify a preferred language. To do this, we need to add a new column to our `users` table.
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN preferred_language VARCHAR(10) NULL;

The keyword `NULL` is critical. It means this new column is optional. Existing rows in the table don't have a value for it, so the database fills it in with a special `NULL` marker, meaning 'no value'.

boardActions.narrationCue: Let's look at our first example from the source, Example A.
Imagine this is our users table before the change. It has an id, name, and email.
We run this ALTER TABLE command to add the new 'preferred_language' column.
After the command executes...
...this is what our table looks like. A new column has appeared.
Notice that for our existing users, Alice and Bob, the database automatically filled the new column with NULL. This is the key to why it's a safe change.

screens.voiceover: Let's look at our first example from the source, Example A: adding a new column. Imagine this is our 'users' table before the change; it just has columns for id, name, and email. The business now wants to store a user's language preference. To do this, we run an `ALTER TABLE` command to add a new `preferred_language` column. After the command executes, our table now has this new column. Most importantly, notice what happened to our existing rows for Alice and Bob. The database automatically filled the new column with a special value: NULL. This means 'no value provided'. This is the secret to why this change is non-destructive, which we'll explore next.
screens.teacherNote: This screen provides a concrete, worked example of the first type of non-destructive change. By showing the 'before' and 'after' state of the data, students can visually grasp the impact of the `ALTER TABLE` command. Highlighting the `NULL` values sets up the next screen, which will explain *why* this makes the change safe.
keyPoints: Adding a column is a common schema change.
The `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN` command is used.
If the new column is `NULL`able, existing rows will be populated with `NULL`.

sourceRef.quote: ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN preferred_language VARCHAR(10) NULL;

screens.checkQuestion: What would happen to a third user, Charlie, if he was in the 'before' table?

screens.title: Why is This Change Safe?
screens.subtitle: It doesn't break old code.

visualElements.content: Labels from the PDF explaining why the change is safe.

Old Application Code (unaware of new column)

SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE id = 1;
Still works!

users
id | name | email | preferred_language
-- | -- | -- | --
1 | Alice | a@b.com | NULL
2 | Bob | c@d.com | NULL

New Application Code (aware of new column)

UPDATE users SET preferred_language = 'en' WHERE id = 1;
Misconception Alert: Many think any `ALTER TABLE` is dangerous. The key is nullability.

blocks.content: The Impact on Code
A change is "non-destructive" if it doesn't force you to immediately rewrite all your existing application code. Let's see why adding a nullable column meets this standard.
Old code that reads from the table still works, because it doesn't ask for the new column.
Old code that writes to the table still works, because the database provides a default (`NULL`) for the missing column.
New code can be written to take advantage of the new column whenever it's ready.

boardActions.narrationCue: The source document gives us three key reasons why this is safe.
First, think about your old application code that was written before the new column existed.
This code might run a SELECT statement asking for just the id and name.
That query still runs perfectly fine! The database just ignores the new column that wasn't asked for.
This buys you time. Later, you can deploy new application code that knows about and uses the new column.
This is a really important point. A common mistake is to think any 'ALTER TABLE' command is destructive, but the details, like nullability, matter.

screens.voiceover: So, why exactly is this change considered safe, or non-destructive? The source document highlights the key reasons. First, think about your old application code, written before this change. It might run a SELECT statement asking for just the user's id and name. That query still works perfectly against the new table structure. The database simply provides the columns that were requested and ignores the new one. This is crucial because it means you don't have to update all your code at once. It buys you time. Later, you can deploy new code that is aware of the `preferred_language` column and can start using it. This is a really important point. A common beginner trap is to think any `ALTER TABLE` command is automatically dangerous, but the details, like whether a new column allows NULLs, are what determine the risk.
screens.teacherNote: This screen directly addresses the 'why'. It connects the database change back to its impact on application code, which is the true measure of whether a change is destructive. It also explicitly calls out and corrects the misconception that `ALTER TABLE` is always destructive.
keyPoints: Non-destructive means old code continues to work without modification.
Existing SELECTs work because they don't request the new column.
Existing INSERTs work because the database supplies a default (NULL) for the new column.
New code can be deployed separately to utilize the new column.
screens.checkQuestion: What would happen if the new column was added with `NOT NULL` and no default value? Would old `INSERT` statements still work?

screens.title: Example B: Adding a New Table
screens.subtitle: The safest change of all.

visualElements.content: The user story and SQL code for adding a new table.

Schema Before:
- users
- products

Schema After:
- users
- products
- wishlists (New!)
CREATE TABLE runs
This is completely isolated. No existing data or code is touched at all.

blocks.content: The second type of non-destructive change.
Example B: Adding a new table (Non-destructive)
Here, the goal is to add a wishlist feature. This requires a completely new table to store which user wants which product.
CREATE TABLE wishlists (...);
This is the safest possible schema change because it doesn't modify any existing tables or data. It's like building a new house on an empty lot—it has no effect on the neighboring houses.

boardActions.narrationCue: Now let's look at the second major type of non-destructive change, Example B from our text.
Imagine our database schema starts with just a 'users' table and a 'products' table.
We then run this `CREATE TABLE` statement to add our new `wishlists` table.
After the command runs...
...our schema now contains the new table. Notice that the 'users' and 'products' tables are completely untouched.
This is a completely isolated operation. No existing code that interacts with users or products is affected in any way.

screens.voiceover: Now let's look at the second major type of non-destructive change, Example B from our text: adding a brand new table. Imagine our database schema starts with just a 'users' table and a 'products' table. To add a wishlist feature, we need a new place to store that data, so we run a `CREATE TABLE` statement to make a `wishlists` table. After the command runs, our schema now contains this new table. Notice that the original 'users' and 'products' tables are completely untouched. This is a completely isolated operation. No existing code that interacts with users or products is affected in any way, making this the safest kind of database change you can make.
screens.teacherNote: This screen introduces the second, and simplest, type of non-destructive change. The visual of the schema 'before' and 'after' makes it clear that the existing objects are not modified. This reinforces the core concept of 'non-destructive' as not interfering with existing structures or code.
keyPoints: Adding a new table is a non-destructive change.
This change is extremely safe because it does not alter any existing tables, data, or code.

sourceRef.quote: Adding a new table is completely safe and does not affect existing tables.

screens.checkQuestion: Why is adding a new table even safer than adding a new nullable column?
